description: The figure displays true stress–true strain compression curves for a Fe-22Mn-0.6C twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) steel tested at seven temperatures spanning 293 K to 873 K. Each temperature condition is represented by a pair of curves: a solid line indicating the simulation prediction and star-shaped markers denoting the experimental measurement. The data show that the flow stress systematically decreases as temperature increases. At lower temperatures (293 K–573 K), the stress rises monotonically with strain, reflecting strong work hardening. At intermediate temperatures (673 K), hardening persists but at a reduced rate. At high temperatures (773 K and 873 K), the curves exhibit a distinct stress peak followed by thermal softening. Overall, the simulations capture the temperature-dependent hardening and softening behavior across the full range using a single coherent parameter set, supporting the validation of the crystal plasticity constitutive model for this low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel system.
